text: In which context is 'shall' used in modern formal English to express obligation?
options: The tenant shall maintain the property in good condition.
Shall we begin the meeting?
I shall never forget this day.
You shall go to the ball!
hint: C1: 'Shall' huquqiy matnlarda majburiyat ifodalaydi.

text: Which sentence demonstrates the correct distinction between 'used to' and 'would' for past habits?
options: When I was a child, I would live in the countryside.
When I was a child, I used to live in the countryside.
When I was a child, I would have lived in the countryside.
When I was a child, I used to living in the countryside.
hint: C1: 'Used to' state va habit uchun, 'would' faqat action habit uchun.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'will' for a future prediction based on a personal opinion rather than evidence?
options: Look at those clouds – it will rain.
I think it will rain tomorrow.
The forecast says it will rain.
It will rain by the time we arrive.
hint: C1: 'I think + will' – shaxsiy fikr, dalilsiz bashorat.

text: Which sentence shows correct subject-verb agreement with a collective noun in British English?
options: The government have announced their decision.
The government has announced their decision.
The government have announced its decision.
The government has announced its decision.
hint: C1: Britaniya ingliz tilida kollektiv ot ko'plik (individual a'zolar).

text: Choose the sentence where 'in case' is used correctly with a past reference.
options: I took an umbrella in case it rains.
I took an umbrella in case it rained.
I took an umbrella in case it will rain.
I took an umbrella in case it would rain.
hint: C1: Pastda 'in case' + past simple.

text: Which sentence correctly distinguishes 'despite' and 'although' with a clause?
options: Despite he was tired, he finished the work.
Despite being tired, he finished the work.
Although being tired, he finished the work.
Despite of being tired, he finished the work.
hint: C1: 'Despite + gerund', 'although + clause'.

text: Which sentence uses 'neither...nor' correctly with three elements?
options: Neither John, Mary, nor Peter came.
Neither John nor Mary nor Peter came.
Neither John, nor Mary, nor Peter came.
Neither John or Mary or Peter came.
hint: C2: 'Neither A nor B nor C' – takrorlanuvchi 'nor'.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'so' and 'such' with a countable noun?
options: It was so beautiful day that we stayed outside.
It was such beautiful day that we stayed outside.
It was so a beautiful day that we stayed outside.
It was such a beautiful day that we stayed outside.
hint: C1: 'Such a/an + adjective + singular countable noun'.

text: Choose the correct reported speech transformation of 'I will help you tomorrow.'
options: He said he will help me tomorrow.
He said he would help me the next day.
He said he would help me tomorrow.
He said he will help me the next day.
hint: C1: 'Will → would', 'tomorrow → the next day'.

text: Which sentence uses 'may' appropriately in a formal written request?
options: May I leave early today?
You may leave if you want.
Students may use the library after 6 PM.
May you help me with this?
hint: C1: 'May' ruxsat uchun – rasmiy yozma qoidalarda.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'can' to express a general possibility?
options: Can you pass the salt?
It can get very cold here in winter.
You can leave now if you wish.
I can speak three languages.
hint: C1: 'Can' umumiy ehtimollik (general possibility).

text: Choose the sentence where 'need' is used as a modal verb in a negative question.
options: Need you make so much noise?
Do you need to make so much noise?
Needn't you make so much noise?
Don't you need to make so much noise?
hint: C2: 'Need you?' – modal 'need' (without 'to') in questions.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'dare' as a semi-modal in a negative statement?
options: I dare not speak the truth.
I don't dare speak the truth.
I dare not to speak the truth.
I don't dare to speak the truth.
hint: C2: 'Dare not + infinitive without to' – semi-modal usage.

text: Which sentence demonstrates the correct use of 'used to' to contrast past and present?
options: I used to smoke, but I don't anymore.
I used to smoke, but I wouldn't anymore.
I used to smoke, but I'm not anymore.
I used to smoke, but I didn't anymore.
hint: C1: 'Used to' past habit vs present contrast.

text: Choose the sentence where 'would' expresses a typical past behavior with irritation.
options: He would always forget his keys.
He used to always forget his keys.
He would have forgotten his keys.
He would be forgetting his keys.
hint: C1: 'Would + always' – typical past behavior (sometimes irritation).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'be to' for a formal future arrangement?
options: The president is to visit France next week.
The president will visit France next week.
The president is going to visit France next week.
The president visits France next week.
hint: C1: 'Be to + infinitive' – rasmiy kelishuv, reja.

text: Which sentence uses 'be about to' correctly for an immediate future?
options: I am about to leave when the phone rang.
I was about to leave when the phone rang.
I am about to have left when the phone rang.
I was about to have left when the phone rang.
hint: C1: 'Was about to' + past event interruption.

text: Choose the sentence where 'will have + past participle' is used correctly for a future perfect prediction.
options: By next year, I will have finished my degree.
By next year, I will finish my degree.
By next year, I have finished my degree.
By next year, I will be finishing my degree.
hint: C1: 'Will have + V3' – future perfect.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'would have + past participle' for a past unreal conditional?
options: If I knew, I would have helped.
If I had known, I would have helped.
If I would have known, I helped.
If I knew, I would help.
hint: C1: 'If + had + V3, would have + V3' – past unreal.

text: Which sentence demonstrates correct use of 'could have + past participle' for a past possibility that didn't happen?
options: You could have told me earlier.
You could tell me earlier.
You could be telling me earlier.
You could have been telling me earlier.
hint: C1: 'Could have + V3' – past possibility (regret/criticism).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'might have + past participle' to express uncertainty about the past?
options: He might have left already.
He might leave already.
He might be leaving already.
He might have been leaving already.
hint: C1: 'Might have + V3' – past uncertainty.

text: Choose the sentence where 'must have + past participle' expresses a logical conclusion about the past.
options: He must have finished by now.
He must finish by now.
He must be finishing by now.
He must have been finishing by now.
hint: C1: 'Must have + V3' – logical deduction about past.

text: Which sentence uses 'can't have + past participle' correctly for negative deduction?
options: He can't have seen us – it was too dark.
He can't see us – it was too dark.
He couldn't have seen us – it was too dark.
He couldn't see us – it was too dark.
hint: C1: 'Can't have + V3' – negative deduction about past.

text: Which sentence demonstrates the correct use of 'needn't have + past participle'?
options: You needn't have bought any bread – we had plenty.
You needn't buy any bread – we had plenty.
You didn't need to buy bread – we had plenty.
You needn't have bought any bread – we have plenty.
hint: C2: 'Needn't have + V3' – unnecessary past action (already done).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'didn't need to' to mean the action was not necessary and was not done?
options: I didn't need to cook because we ate out.
I needn't have cooked because we ate out.
I didn't need to cook but I did.
I needn't cook because we ate out.
hint: C2: 'Didn't need to' – action not necessary and not done.

text: Choose the sentence where 'had better' expresses a strong warning rather than advice.
options: You had better finish your homework.
You had better not be late again.
You had better see a doctor.
You had better take an umbrella.
hint: C1: 'Had better not' – strong warning (threat).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'would rather' with a past reference?
options: I would rather you had not told him.
I would rather you didn't tell him.
I would rather you not tell him.
I would rather you haven't told him.
hint: C2: 'Would rather + subject + had + V3' – past regret.

text: Which sentence correctly distinguishes 'like' and 'as' in a comparison?
options: He speaks like a native speaker does.
He speaks as a native speaker.
He speaks like a native speaker.
He speaks as like a native speaker.
hint: C1: 'Like' + noun (comparison), 'as' + clause.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'as if' for an unreal situation in the present?
options: He talks as if he knows everything.
He talks as if he knew everything.
He talks as if he has known everything.
He talks as if he is knowing everything.
hint: C1: 'As if + past subjunctive' – unreal present.

text: Choose the sentence where 'even if' introduces a hypothetical concession.
options: Even if it rains, we will go.
Even if it rained, we would go.
Even if it had rained, we would have gone.
All of the above.
hint: C1: 'Even if' – hypothetical concession in all time frames.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'only if' for a strong condition?
options: Only if you apologize will I forgive you.
Only if you apologize I will forgive you.
Only if you apologize, I will forgive you.
Only if you apologize will I forgive you?
hint: C1: 'Only if + clause + auxiliary + subject' – inversion.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'provided that' with a future reference?
options: Provided that you will come, I'll stay.
Provided that you come, I'll stay.
Provided that you came, I'll stay.
Provided that you have come, I'll stay.
hint: C1: 'Provided that + present simple' for future condition.

text: Choose the sentence where 'suppose' introduces a hypothetical situation.
options: Suppose you won the lottery, what would you do?
Suppose you win the lottery, what will you do?
Suppose you have won the lottery, what will you do?
Suppose you are winning the lottery, what will you do?
hint: C1: 'Suppose + past tense' for hypothetical.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'what if' for a worrying possibility?
options: What if he doesn't come?
What if he didn't come?
What if he hadn't come?
What if he won't come?
hint: C1: 'What if + present simple' – real worry.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'as long as' for a condition?
options: You can stay as long as you are quiet.
You can stay as long as you will be quiet.
You can stay as long as you were quiet.
You can stay as long as you have been quiet.
hint: C1: 'As long as + present simple' – condition.

text: Which sentence demonstrates the correct use of 'on condition that' with a negative?
options: I'll lend you the money on condition that you not waste it.
I'll lend you the money on condition that you don't waste it.
I'll lend you the money on condition that you won't waste it.
I'll lend you the money on condition that you didn't waste it.
hint: C2: 'On condition that + don't' for negative condition.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'but for' in a past conditional?
options: But for your help, I would have failed.
But for your help, I would fail.
But for your help, I failed.
But for your help, I have failed.
hint: C2: 'But for + noun, would have + V3' – past conditional.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'if it were not for' in a present context?
options: If it were not for your help, I would be lost.
If it were not for your help, I would have been lost.
If it had not been for your help, I would be lost.
If it was not for your help, I would be lost.
hint: C2: 'If it were not for' + would – present unreal.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'if only' to express a past wish?
options: If only I had studied harder.
If only I studied harder.
If only I study harder.
If only I will study harder.
hint: C1: 'If only + had + V3' – past regret.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'it's time' with a past subjunctive?
options: It's time we went home.
It's time we go home.
It's time we will go home.
It's time we have gone home.
hint: C1: 'It's time + past subjunctive' – suggestion for now.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'it's high time' for criticism?
options: It's high time you started working harder.
It's high time you start working harder.
It's high time you will start working harder.
It's high time you have started working harder.
hint: C1: 'It's high time + past subjunctive' – criticism.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'for fear that' with 'might'?
options: He left early for fear that he might miss the train.
He left early for fear that he will miss the train.
He left early for fear that he misses the train.
He left early for fear that he missed the train.
hint: C1: 'For fear that + might' – past concern.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'lest' with the subjunctive?
options: He ran lest he be late.
He ran lest he is late.
He ran lest he was late.
He ran lest he will be late.
hint: C2: 'Lest + subjunctive (be)' – formal negative purpose.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in case of' with a noun phrase?
options: In case of fire, leave the building.
In case of fire, you will leave the building.
In case of fire, leaving the building.
In case of fire, to leave the building.
hint: C1: 'In case of + noun' – emergency instructions.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in the event of' formally?
options: In the event of an emergency, call 911.
In the event of an emergency, you will call 911.
In the event of an emergency, calling 911.
In the event of an emergency, called 911.
hint: C1: 'In the event of + noun' – formal contingency.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'with a view to' with a gerund?
options: He bought the land with a view to building a house.
He bought the land with a view to build a house.
He bought the land with a view to built a house.
He bought the land with a view to will build a house.
hint: C1: 'With a view to + V-ing' – purpose.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'so as to' with a negative infinitive?
options: He left early so as not to miss the train.
He left early so as to not miss the train.
He left early so as to don't miss the train.
He left early so as to won't miss the train.
hint: C1: 'So as not to + infinitive' – negative purpose.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in order that' with 'might'?
options: He spoke slowly in order that everyone might understand.
He spoke slowly in order that everyone will understand.
He spoke slowly in order that everyone understands.
He spoke slowly in order that everyone understood.
hint: C1: 'In order that + might' – formal purpose.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'seeing that' for an obvious reason?
options: Seeing that it's raining, we should stay home.
Seeing that it rains, we should stay home.
Seeing that it rained, we should stay home.
Seeing that it will rain, we should stay home.
hint: C1: 'Seeing that + present' – obvious reason.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'given that' for a known fact?
options: Given that he is an expert, his opinion matters.
Given that he was an expert, his opinion mattered.
Given that he will be an expert, his opinion will matter.
Given that he has been an expert, his opinion has mattered.
hint: C1: 'Given that + present' – known fact.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in that' for a specific reason in academic writing?
options: The theory is flawed in that it ignores historical context.
The theory is flawed in that, it ignores historical context.
The theory is flawed in that which ignores historical context.
The theory is flawed in that where it ignores historical context.
hint: C1: 'In that + clause' – specific reason (academic).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'now that' for a new situation?
options: Now that you are here, we can start.
Now that you were here, we could start.
Now that you will be here, we will start.
Now that you have been here, we started.
hint: C1: 'Now that + present' – new situation.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'insofar as' to limit a claim?
options: He is correct insofar as I can judge.
He is correct insofar as I can judge?
He is correct insofar as I can judge!
He is correct insofar as I can judge;
hint: C2: 'Insofar as' – to the extent that (formal).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'for all' to mean 'despite'?
options: For all his wealth, he is unhappy.
For all his wealth he is unhappy.
For all his wealth, but he is unhappy.
For all his wealth, so he is unhappy.
hint: C2: 'For all + noun' = despite.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'much as' for concession?
options: Much as I admire him, I cannot agree.
Much as I admire him I cannot agree.
Much as I admire him, but I cannot agree.
Much as I admire him, so I cannot agree.
hint: C2: 'Much as + clause' = although.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'be that as it may' for concession?
options: Be that as it may, we must continue.
Be that as it may we must continue.
Be that as it may, but we must continue.
Be that as it may, so we must continue.
hint: C2: 'Be that as it may' – formal concession.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'nevertheless' with a semicolon?
options: It was raining; nevertheless, we went out.
It was raining nevertheless, we went out.
It was raining, nevertheless we went out.
It was raining nevertheless we went out.
hint: C1: '; nevertheless,' – formal contrast.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'nonetheless' for contrast?
options: It was difficult; nonetheless, we succeeded.
It was difficult nonetheless, we succeeded.
It was difficult, nonetheless we succeeded.
It was difficult nonetheless we succeeded.
hint: C1: '; nonetheless,' – formal contrast.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'notwithstanding' in postpositive position?
options: The difficulties notwithstanding, we succeeded.
Notwithstanding the difficulties, we succeeded.
We succeeded, the difficulties notwithstanding.
We succeeded notwithstanding the difficulties.
hint: C2: 'Noun + notwithstanding' – most formal.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'regardless of' with a gerund?
options: Regardless of being tired, he finished.
Regardless of he was tired, he finished.
Regardless of his tiredness, he finished.
Regardless of tired, he finished.
hint: C1: 'Regardless of + gerund' – despite.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'irrespective of' with a noun?
options: Irrespective of age, everyone can apply.
Irrespective of age everyone can apply.
Irrespective of age, everyone can apply
Irrespective of age everyone can apply.
hint: C1: 'Irrespective of + noun' – regardless of.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in light of' with a past event?
options: In light of recent events, we changed our plans.
In light of recent events we changed our plans.
In light of recent events, we change our plans.
In light of recent events we change our plans.
hint: C1: 'In light of + noun' – because of (past result).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'owing to' in formal writing?
options: Owing to the rain, the match was cancelled.
Owing to the rain the match was cancelled.
Owing to the rain, the match is cancelled.
Owing to the rain the match is cancelled.
hint: C1: 'Owing to + noun' – because of (formal).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'thanks to' for a positive reason?
options: Thanks to your help, we succeeded.
Thanks to your help we succeeded.
Thanks to your help, we succeed.
Thanks to your help we succeed.
hint: C1: 'Thanks to + noun' – positive cause.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'by virtue of' in a legal context?
options: He holds power by virtue of his position.
He holds power by virtue of his position?
He holds power by virtue of his position!
He holds power by virtue of his position;
hint: C2: 'By virtue of + noun' – because of (legal/formal).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in view of' with a future plan?
options: In view of the weather, we will stay home.
In view of the weather we will stay home.
In view of the weather, we stay home.
In view of the weather we stay home.
hint: C1: 'In view of + noun' – considering.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'on account of' with a gerund?
options: He was late on account of missing the bus.
He was late on account of he missed the bus.
He was late on account of his missing the bus.
He was late on account of missed the bus.
hint: C1: 'On account of + gerund' – because of.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in consequence of' formally?
options: In consequence of his actions, he was fired.
In consequence of his actions he was fired.
In consequence of his actions, he is fired.
In consequence of his actions he is fired.
hint: C2: 'In consequence of + noun' – as a result of.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'as a result of' with a noun phrase?
options: As a result of the storm, many trees fell.
As a result of the storm many trees fell.
As a result of the storm, many trees fall.
As a result of the storm many trees fall.
hint: C1: 'As a result of + noun' – cause-effect.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'due to' after a form of 'to be'?
options: The cancellation was due to the rain.
Due to the rain, the game was cancelled.
The game was cancelled due to the rain.
All of the above.
hint: C1: 'Due to' – all positions acceptable now.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'the same...as' with an inverted clause?
options: He has the same car as does his brother.
He has the same car as his brother does.
He has the same car as his brother.
All of the above.
hint: C2: 'The same...as' – inversion possible.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'different from' with a pronoun?
options: His opinion is different from mine.
His opinion is different than mine.
His opinion is different to mine.
His opinion is different of mine.
hint: C1: 'Different from' – standard English.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'similar to' with a clause?
options: His approach is similar to that of his predecessor.
His approach is similar to his predecessor.
His approach is similar like his predecessor.
His approach is similar as his predecessor.
hint: C1: 'Similar to that of' – precise comparison.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'such as' with a list?
options: He likes sports such as football, tennis, and basketball.
He likes sports such as football, tennis and basketball.
He likes sports such as: football, tennis, basketball.
He likes sports such as football tennis basketball.
hint: C1: 'Such as' – comma before list, 'and' before last.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'rather than' with parallel structure?
options: He chose to quit rather than to continue.
He chose to quit rather than continue.
He chose quitting rather than continuing.
All of the above.
hint: C1: 'Rather than' – parallel structures.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'no less than' for emphasis?
options: He gave no less than $1000.
He gave no less than $1000?
He gave no less than $1000!
He gave no less than $1000;
hint: C1: 'No less than' – emphatic quantity.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'nothing less than' for strong emotion?
options: His behavior was nothing less than outrageous.
His behavior was nothing less than outrageous?
His behavior was nothing less than outrageous!
His behavior was nothing less than outrageous;
hint: C2: 'Nothing less than' – completely (strong).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'all but' to mean 'almost'?
options: He was all but dead when they found him.
He was all but dead when they found him?
He was all but dead when they found him!
He was all but dead when they found him;
hint: C1: 'All but + adjective' = almost.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'anything but' for negation?
options: He was anything but happy.
He was anything but happy?
He was anything but happy!
He was anything but happy;
hint: C1: 'Anything but + adjective' = not at all.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'nothing but' for restriction?
options: He wanted nothing but success.
He wanted nothing but success?
He wanted nothing but success!
He wanted nothing but success;
hint: C1: 'Nothing but + noun' = only.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'more than' with an adjective for emphasis?
options: He is more than happy to help.
He is more than happy to help?
He is more than happy to help!
He is more than happy to help;
hint: C1: 'More than + adjective' = very.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'sooner or later' for inevitability?
options: Sooner or later, everyone makes mistakes.
Sooner or later everyone makes mistakes.
Sooner or later, everyone makes mistakes
Sooner or later everyone makes mistakes.
hint: C1: 'Sooner or later' – inevitable future.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'as it were' as a hedging device?
options: He is, as it were, a walking encyclopedia.
He is as it were a walking encyclopedia.
He is, as it were a walking encyclopedia.
He is as it were, a walking encyclopedia.
hint: C2: 'As it were' – hedging (formal).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'so to speak' for figurative language?
options: He is, so to speak, a lone wolf.
He is so to speak a lone wolf.
He is, so to speak a lone wolf.
He is so to speak, a lone wolf.
hint: C2: 'So to speak' – figurative marker.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in a manner of speaking'?
options: He is, in a manner of speaking, the leader.
He is in a manner of speaking the leader.
He is, in a manner of speaking the leader.
He is in a manner of speaking, the leader.
hint: C2: 'In a manner of speaking' – loosely speaking.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'to say the least' for understatement?
options: His behavior was inappropriate, to say the least.
His behavior was inappropriate to say the least.
His behavior was inappropriate, to say the least
His behavior was inappropriate to say the least.
hint: C2: 'To say the least' – understatement.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'needless to say'?
options: Needless to say, he was late again.
Needless to say he was late again.
Needless to say, he was late again
Needless to say he was late again.
hint: C1: 'Needless to say' – obvious statement.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'strange as it may seem'?
options: Strange as it may seem, he passed the test.
Strange as it may seem he passed the test.
Strange as it may seem, he passed the test
Strange as it may seem he passed the test.
hint: C2: 'Strange as it may seem' – concession.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'believe it or not'?
options: Believe it or not, I have never seen snow.
Believe it or not I have never seen snow.
Believe it or not, I have never seen snow
Believe it or not I have never seen snow.
hint: C1: 'Believe it or not' – surprising truth.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'if you ask me' for opinion?
options: If you ask me, he is making a mistake.
If you ask me he is making a mistake.
If you ask me, he is making a mistake
If you ask me he is making a mistake.
hint: C1: 'If you ask me' – personal opinion.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'mind you' to add a contrasting point?
options: He is very smart. Mind you, he can be lazy.
He is very smart. Mind you he can be lazy.
He is very smart mind you, he can be lazy.
He is very smart mind you he can be lazy.
hint: C2: 'Mind you' – contrast marker.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'still' as a discourse marker for contrast?
options: He is rich. Still, he is unhappy.
He is rich still, he is unhappy.
He is rich still he is unhappy.
He is rich, still he is unhappy.
hint: C1: 'Still' – contrast marker (nonetheless).

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'though' at the end of a sentence for contrast?
options: He is rich. He is unhappy, though.
He is rich. He is unhappy though.
He is rich. Though, he is unhappy.
He is rich though, he is unhappy.
hint: C1: 'Though' at end – informal contrast.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'whereas' to contrast two clauses?
options: He likes coffee, whereas she prefers tea.
He likes coffee whereas she prefers tea.
He likes coffee whereas, she prefers tea.
He likes coffee, whereas she prefers tea.
hint: C1: 'Whereas' – direct contrast.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'while' for contrast (not time)?
options: While he is rich, she is poor.
While he is rich she is poor.
While he is rich, she is poor
While he is rich she is poor.
hint: C1: 'While' for contrast – comma.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'on the contrary' after a negative?
options: He is not lazy. On the contrary, he works very hard.
He is not lazy. On the contrary he works very hard.
He is not lazy on the contrary, he works very hard.
He is not lazy on the contrary he works very hard.
hint: C1: 'On the contrary' – after negative.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'in contrast' with 'to'?
options: In contrast to his brother, he is tall.
In contrast with his brother, he is tall.
In contrast of his brother, he is tall.
In contrast for his brother, he is tall.
hint: C1: 'In contrast to + noun' – standard.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'by contrast' without 'to'?
options: He is tall. By contrast, his brother is short.
He is tall. By contrast his brother is short.
He is tall by contrast, his brother is short.
He is tall by contrast his brother is short.
hint: C1: 'By contrast' – no 'to'.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'conversely' for opposite relationship?
options: Increasing price reduces demand. Conversely, lowering price increases demand.
Increasing price reduces demand. Conversely lowering price increases demand.
Increasing price reduces demand conversely, lowering price increases demand.
Increasing price reduces demand conversely lowering price increases demand.
hint: C1: 'Conversely' – opposite logical relationship.

text: Which sentence correctly uses 'as opposed to' for clear distinction?
options: He prefers coffee as opposed to tea.
He prefers coffee as opposed to tea?
He prefers coffee as opposed to tea!
He prefers coffee as opposed to tea;
hint: C1: 'As opposed to' – explicit contrast.
